~q","logical-implication-truth|Truth Condition of Material Implication|An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true. Equivalently X -> Y equals ~X v Y.","logical-equivalence-truth-table|Logical Equivalence via Truth Tables|Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table; a single differing row disproves equivalence.","demorgan-law|De Morgan's Laws|Negating a conjunction flips it to a disjunction of negations, and vice versa. listing those rows gives the function's truth vector directly.","de-morgans-law|De Morgan's Laws|not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B); not(A or B) = (not A) and (not B)","only-if-implication|'Only If' as Implication|'A only if B' means A -> B (B is necessary for A)","when-implication|'When' Clause as Implication|'Y when X' means X -> Y (the 'when' clause is the antecedent)","distributive-law|Distributive Law (and over or)|A and (B or C) == (A and B) or (A and C)","self-implication-tautology|Self-Implication Tautology|X -> X is always True for any formula X","context-implication-simplify|Antecedent-Context Simplification of an Implication|If the antecedent A guarantees Q, then A -> (Q -> R) is equivalent to A -> R","vacuous-truth|Vacuous Truth (False Antecedent)|An implication with a False antecedent is automatically True","argument-validity-tautology|Validity of a Propositional Argument (Tautology Test)|An argument with premises P_1,...,P_n and conclusion C is valid iff the conditional (P_1 /\\ ... /\\ P_n) -> C is true under every truth assignment; equivalently, there is no assignment making all premises true and C false.","commutative-binary-op-count|Number of Commutative Binary Operations on an n-Element Set|the number of commutative binary operations on a set of n elements is n^(n(n+1)/2), since the symmetric operation table has n(n+1)/2 freely chosen cells (diagonal plus upper triangle) each filled with one of n values","associative-binary-operation|Associativity of a Binary Operation|a binary operation * is associative when regrouping the operands does not change the result, i.e. (a*b)*c equals a*(b*c) for all elements a, b, c","commutative-property|Commutative Property of a Binary Operation|a * b = b * a for all a, b","associative-property|Associative Property of a Binary Operation|(a * b) * c = a * (b * c) for all a, b, c","identity-element-binary-op|Identity Element of a Binary Operation|z is an identity if f(a, z) = a = f(z, a) for every a","xor-operator|XOR (Exclusive OR) Operator|p XOR q = 1 when p and q differ, equal to (p + q) mod 2","nand-operator|NAND Operator (X' + Y')|X NAND Y = X' + Y' = complement of (X AND Y)","fol-prenex-bounded-model|Bounded Model Size from Existential Witnesses (Prenex FOL)|A prenex first-order sentence with no function symbols and k essential existential witnesses has a model of size at most k (universals can reuse the witness elements).","fol-divisibility-prime-def|Divisor Characterization of a Prime|An integer n is prime exactly when its only divisors are 1 and n itself (1 satisfies the clause vacuously).","fol-quantifier-distribution|Quantifier Distribution over Connectives (constant W)|When W has no free x: forall distributes over v as forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W; exists distributes over ^ as exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W; and in an implication the antecedent quantifier flips, so forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W.","fol-implication-validity|Semantic Implication Between FOL Statements|Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.","exclusive-or-definition|Exclusive-Or Definition|p XOR q is true exactly when p and q differ; it equals (not p and q) or (p and not q) and is the negation of the biconditional","logical-implication-entailment|Logical Implication (Entailment)|F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true","vacuous-implication|Vacuous Truth of Implication|an implication with a false antecedent is automatically true (false -> anything = true)","countable-subset-closure|Countability is Closed Under Subsets and Countable Unions|any subset of a countable set is countable; a countable union of countable sets is countable","cantor-power-set-uncountable|Cantor: Functions N -> {0,1} (Power Set of N) are Uncountable|the set of functions from N to {0,1} (equivalently the power set of N, or infinite binary strings) is uncountable","onto-implies-one-to-one-finite|On a Finite Set, Onto Self-Map Implies One-to-One|for a finite set A, a surjective function f: A -> A is automatically injective (and vice versa), so it is a bijection","image-of-preimage-subset|Image of a Preimage is Contained in the Set|f[ f^{-1}[D] ] subseteq D  (with equality on D intersect range(f))","unique-factorization-encoding|Unique Factorization (Prime-Power / Godel Encoding)|N = prod p_i^{e_i} uniquely encodes the exponent list (e_1, e_2, ...)","composition-onto-forces-outer-onto|Onto Composition Forces the Outer Function Onto|if h = f o g is onto then f is onto; range(f o g) subseteq range(f)","double-counting-incidences|Double Counting of Element-Set Incidences|sum_i f(i) = sum_j |X_j| = number of (i, j) with i in X_j","count-boolean-functions|Number of Boolean Functions of n Variables|number of Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n)","surjective-functions-count|Number of Onto (Surjective) Functions|Onto functions from an m-set onto a k-set = sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i C(k,i) (k-i)^m = k! * S(m,k)","preimage-intersection-distributes|Preimage Distributes over Intersection|f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T) for every function f","involution-function|Involution (Self-Inverse Function)|f(f(i)) = i means f is its own inverse: a bijection made of 2-cycles and fixed points","function-composition|Function Composition|(g o h)(x) = g(h(x)) — substitute h(x) into g wherever its variable appears","group-axioms|Group Axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse)|A set G with operation * is a group if it is closed, associative, has an identity e, and every element has an inverse; thus this identity holds for all pairs precisely in an abelian group.","probability-equally-likely|Probability with Equally Likely Outcomes|P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)","bijection-equal-cardinality|Bijection Exists If and Only If Equal Cardinality (Finite Sets)|two finite sets admit a bijection exactly when they have the same number of elements; a bijection is also a surjection and an injection","function-image-equivalence-classes|Image Size Bounded by Forced-Equality Classes|if functional rules force f to take equal values on linked inputs, the number of distinct values f can take is at most the number of equivalence classes those links create","kernel-quotient-bijection|Canonical Bijection from the Quotient by f's Kernel onto its Image|for the relation a~b iff f(a)=f(b), the induced map F([x]) = f(x) is well-defined and one-to-one; the inner map f need not be onto","shannon-expansion|Shannon's Expansion of a Boolean Function|any boolean function can be expanded about a variable x_i as f = x_i' f(...,x_i=0,...) + x_i f(...,x_i=1,...); the literals x_i' and x_i act as selectors that pick the correct cofactor","group-cancellation-law|Cancellation Laws in a Group|in any group you may cancel a common factor: left cancellation says a*x = a*y implies x = y, and right cancellation says x*a = y*a implies x = y, because every element has an inverse","algebraic-structure-hierarchy|Hierarchy of Algebraic Structures|the standard algebraic structures form a ladder where each adds one axiom: a semigroup has an associative operation, a monoid adds an identity, a group adds inverses, and an abelian group adds commutativity","involution-equiv-order-two|Self-Inverse Element (Involution)|an element a of a group is its own inverse exactly when a^2 = e;
